I read a couple of interesting stories today, concerning President Trump as he pertains to comedy in these still United States of America.
The first was the matter of the Correspondent's Dinner, an event held every year in the nation's capitol.
The President kills, then the comedian bombs, affirming an age-old showbiz maxim, namely: Never follow the leader of the free world.
The second story was the announcement that NBC was considering a Thursday night half-hour prime-time version of Weekend Update.
I have anchored Weekend Update and I have been the featured comedian at the Correspondent's Dinner.
So there are two stories to talk about. Let us discuss the second one second, just to be different.
The story of the Correspondent's Dinner and its choice of a comedian is one that contains several components.
So let us unpack them, as people who like new made-up words, like to say.
First, who would the President like to see take the podium? A comedian who outright hates him, John Oliver, Samantha Bee, Stephen Colbert.
Mr. Colbert announced he would like to do it.
The choice of Mr. Colbert presents two major problems.
For those who loved the last time he hosted, remember this will not be "Stephen Colbert" who will show up, but rather Stephen Colbert.
Once the quotation marks disappear so does any chance at satire.
The second problem is that the last time Mr. Colbert performed, no one laughed.
That does not mean it wasn't funny.
But in my experienced as a comedian, once you've done a gig where no one in the room laughed, you are not invited back.
Further, it is hard to imagine President Trump allowing any comedian who hates him, follow him.
It is not that he is threatened by an enemy, but I doubt he would allow the enemy the final word.
Your slow but inexorable transfiguration into the v... — There's some Gorbachev in there, too. https://t.co/1sn8Axmbbc
If it was reversed, I have no doubt President Trump would be fine with following a seasoned comic with jokes written 3 months in advance.
I have no doubt President Trump would improvise for twenty minutes and I have no doubt President Trump would bury him (her).
Let's keep unpacking.
Would President Trump be happy with a comedian like Dennis Miller, brilliantly funny,and able to deliver a monologue easier on the Commander
President Trump knows, of course, that he would be pilloried for having a comic who didn't downright hate him.
Unpacking is kinda fun, right?
Now, what about looking at it from the comic's perspective. Would simply showing up not be seen as legitimizing a legitimate President?
The Correspondent's Dinner is a normal event and would proceeding with a normal event with President Trump attending not normalize the event
If something like that were to happen, the American people might get the crazy idea that they were leading normal lives.
Now, the comedian would be seen by more people than all the other comics who have performed at Correspondent's Dinner's before, combined.
Reading you talk about fatness, mental illness and ... — Well personally I would have probably become a monk... https://t.co/JJeEDsevch
The more viciously he (she) were to attack President Trump, the more fawning the media would treat him( or her).
Like so many others who vilify President Trump, the comedian could become a star thanks to the object of his vilification.
He would owe a thank-you to a President he may downright hate.
But anything less than a savage takedown and the comedian is a quisling, finding himself at a CPAC convention, opening for Victoria Jackson.
Gorbachev, very foolish no? Kind of just pulled the... — The fundamental problem was that Gorbachev thought ... https://t.co/rdBemoQNpI
This unpacking stuff is getting really exhausting. Just like real unpacking.
Now comes the question if Trump even shows up for the thing.
The idea of President Trump appearing before a roomful of media to be mocked is akin to President Trump holding a press conference.
And, so, what if the comic shows up to make fun of the President alone. Would his laughs mean anything?
And, furthermore, would the knowledge that President Trump was at home gleefully critiquing every joke rattle the designated comic?
There is another option for the comic but let me gat to that after the second of the two stories.
Weekend Update is considering a Thursday half-hour prime-time edition, it was reported today.
NBC says the segment is reporting its highest ratings in 22 years.
The last time the numbers were higher was 1995 and 1996, said the Peacock Network Proudly.
I anchored Weekend Update in both 1995 and 1996, where I coined the term "fake news" among many other terms. I love coining.
Slain woman's mom writes angry letter to Trump after daughter's killing was on White House list of terror attacks. http://cnn.it/2lpLPEr
My last post may appear at first blush a "humblebrag" but it is not. It is a straight-out brag.
The motive for the prime-time edition is clear to all but the most naive. It is the same motive as for all else in this venal world.
It is money.
God bless Lorne Michaels. He is a gentleman and what he has created is priceless. Every dime he has he has earned.
When you get higher up, to the fellows (and ladies) who run NBC, then the ethics get murky to the point of black.
help
SNL will make money off President Trump,the man who was asked to host SNL, did so, then said goodnight alone as performers walked away.
Take the money, guys, but for God's sake, do the decent thing and shake the man's hand on the way out. Whores everywhere else do it.
And you are SNL. Please do not cloak this move as shining a light on what you perceive to be a dark President and his dark policies.
It is not that.
It is a cheap money grab.
I am all for it but it is a cheap money grab.
As to the Correspondent's dinner, an idea occurred to me this afternoon, an idea as to how best approach this thing as a comedian.
It is a very good idea. Not softball nor hardball, more screwball.
The President would like it, the folks at home would like it, and all but the far fringes of the media would heartily approve.
When they choose whoever the hot comedian of the day is, I think I will tell him (or her) this idea.
Or perhaps I will not, and instead sit back at home and watch what is fated to be, in the word of the President, nothing short of Carnage.
Peace.
